# 2026 California Employment Law Update: AB5 and Independent Contractors — JDSupra / Maynard Nexsen

## Summary
2026 law firm analysis of California worker classification law, covering the current state of AB5 (effective January 1, 2020), the ABC test, the 2026 SB 809 amendment (vehicle reimbursement and construction trucking amnesty), and the Borello test exemptions. Critical compliance reference for any client hiring workers in California.

## Key quotations / statistics
- AB5 effective date: January 1, 2020; codified the "ABC test" that presumes all workers are employees unless employers prove otherwise
- **The ABC Test — all three prongs must be satisfied for contractor status:**
  - Factor A (Control): "Worker is free from control and direction of the hiring entity in performance of work"
  - Factor B (Usual Business): "Work is outside the hiring entity's usual course of business" — considered the hardest prong to satisfy for software/tech companies
  - Factor C (Independent Trade): "Worker is customarily engaged in an independently established trade, occupation, or business of the same nature"
- "If any factor fails, the worker must be classified as an employee with full employment protections"
- **2026 SB 809 (effective January 1, 2026) adds:**
  - Vehicle ownership does not make someone an independent contractor; the ABC test still applies
  - Employers must reimburse employees for work-related vehicle expenses (use, maintenance, depreciation)
  - Construction trucking amnesty program: settle misclassification claims by January 1, 2029
- **Exemptions from AB5 (governed by the alternative Borello test instead):** Licensed insurance agents, healthcare providers, attorneys, real estate licensees, direct sales workers, construction subcontractors

## Annotations for stinger-forge
- The AB5 ABC test with Factor B as the hardest prong is the most practically important content for `guides/06-compliance-hotspots.md` — tech companies hiring CA-based contractors to do core engineering work routinely fail Factor B
- SB 809's vehicle reimbursement requirement (effective January 1, 2026) is a new 2026 compliance item — add to the "2026 updates" section of the compliance hotspots guide
- Stinger-forge should emphasize that CA law applies to any worker physically located in California, regardless of where the company is headquartered
- The Borello test exemptions list is important — if a founder asks about a specific profession (e.g., an attorney or licensed healthcare worker), the ABC test may not apply
- Link to `guides/02-classification-matrix.md`: when the worker is in California, the California ABC test applies in addition to the federal IRS common law test — both must be satisfied for contractor status
